name: Sichuan Basin China

staple_foods.name: Sichuan pepper (huajiao)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 2000-4000 mg GAE/100g [Zanthoxylum bungeanum — Liu et al. 2017 Food Chem 221:1679]

terpenes.linalool: 15-35 g/100g EO [Sichuan pepper essential oil — linalool is dominant at 20-40%; Liu et al. 2017 Food Chem 221:1679; highest linalool in the pilot set by far]
terpenes.limonene: 5-10 g/100g EO [Sichuan pepper EO — Liu et al. 2017]
terpenes.myrcene: 2-5 g/100g EO [Sichuan pepper — Liu et al. 2017]
terpenes.pinene: 2-4 g/100g EO [alpha-pinene in Sichuan pepper — Liu et al. 2017]

staple_foods.notes: Sichuan pepper is NOT related to black pepper or chili — it is a citrus-family (Rutaceae) pericarp that causes oral tingling/numbness (ma, 麻) through hydroxy-alpha-sanshool, a compound that activates tactile mechano-receptors. It has zero capsaicin (confirmed absent). The linalool content of the essential oil (15-35 g/100g EO) is the highest in the pilot set — Sichuan pepper is essentially concentrated linalool in dried pericarp form. The ma-la (numb-spicy) flavor system (Sichuan pepper + chili) is a unique dual-channel activation not represented elsewhere. This RSU introduces the most terpene-rich aromatic in the pilot set.
data_sources: Liu et al. (2017) — Chemical composition of Sichuan pepper EO, Food Chem 221:1679

staple_foods.name: doubanjiang (fermented bean paste)

key_flavor_bioactives.capsaicinoids: 1.0-5.0 mg/g [Pixian doubanjiang — chili component; Sichuan chili varieties added during fermentation]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 200-500 mg GAE/100g [doubanjiang fermented phenolics — Aspergillus oryzae metabolism of soy isoflavones]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.8-2.0 g/100g free glutamate [Pixian doubanjiang — 3-5 year aged; Aspergillus oryzae protease activity; Jiang et al. 2018 Food Chem 259:217]
umami_compounds.inosinate: trace [fermented bean paste — minimal IMP in plant-based ferment]

staple_foods.notes: Pixian doubanjiang (Pixian fermented broad bean paste with chili) is the 'soul of Sichuan cuisine' — aged 3-5 years in outdoor clay urns using Aspergillus oryzae (similar to miso koji but with chili). The combination of high free glutamate (umami from koji proteolysis) and capsaicin (from chili) in a single fermented product is unique in the pilot set. Compare to miso (RSU-22) for the koji-fermented glutamate dimension; compare to harissa (RSU-15) and gochujang (RSU-06) for chili-fermented paste dimension.
data_sources: Jiang et al. (2018) — Flavor compounds in doubanjiang, Food Chem 259:217

staple_foods.name: rice (Sichuan short/medium grain)
staple_foods.notes: Sichuan Basin rice is the foundational carbohydrate — the basin's humid subtropical climate and flat fertile land supports intensive rice cultivation. The Sichuan food system is rice-based, not wheat-based (unlike northern China RSU-13/14).

staple_foods.name: pork belly (for hong shao rou)
staple_foods.notes: Pork belly (hong shao rou, red-braised) is the defining protein of Sichuan and broader Han Chinese cuisine. Fat-rich cuts are culturally prized. Sichuan cuisine uses pork in countless preparations — mapo tofu (minced pork + doubanjiang + Sichuan pepper) is the single most famous Sichuan dish.
data_sources: Maga (1983) — Flavor potentiators, CRC Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr 18:231

staple_foods.name: rapeseed oil (Sichuan)
staple_foods.notes: Sichuan is one of China's largest rapeseed (canola) producing regions — the bright yellow rapeseed flower carpets are iconic. Traditionally, Sichuan rapeseed oil was high-erucic (pre-canola variety) but modern varieties are low-erucic. Used as the primary cooking fat in all Sichuan stir-frying and chili oil production.

notes: Sichuan Basin, southwestern China (~500m altitude). Sedimentary basin surrounded by mountains with humid subtropical climate — low sunlight (overcast year-round), high humidity. The Sichuan food system is defined by the ma-la (numb-spicy) flavor axis: Sichuan pepper linalool/sanshool + chili capsaicin. Sichuan pepper (RSU-30) has the highest linalool concentration of any single food in the pilot set (15-35 g/100g of EO), dwarfing all other linalool sources (coffee 0.8-2.1 mg/L, tea 15-35 mg/kg, lavender-type herbs). The doubanjiang combines capsaicin + glutamate in a single fermented paste — no other fermented product in the pilot set does this. Compare to RSU-13/14 (Shanxi China) and RSU-26 (Yunnan China) for multi-RSU Chinese food system coverage.
